turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: May it please the Court. This is an action involving the right of 1377 Negroes to be restored to the registration rolls of Washington Parish in Louisiana. It is distinguishable from Raines in that Raines involved the question of their being placed upon the registration rolls in the State of Georgia. In this case, the District Judge, in his decree, held that the action involved massive discrimination against these Negro voters. The various acts that were involved resulted from challenges under the statute of Louisiana. The statute of Louisiana provides for challenges in a certain manner and that is set out in a Government's brief. The challenges maybe made by persons, voters who will swear out affidavits in regard to the discrepancy in a form that is described in the statute and it's exhibit here in our brief, in which this discrepancies are raised. Then the Registrar acts upon those affidavits which are to be sworn out before the Registrar or his deputy.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: There's two affidavit as I understand (Inaudible) --

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: At least two voters.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: -- or not have been done by voters.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: That's right Mr. Justice Whittaker. In this case, if the record is clear, it's submitted that no examination of any ward in regard to whether voters were in compliance with the requirements of the registration laws was made where there were no Negro voters in that ward. There were only 10 white voters' registrations, who were challenged in any way in Washington Parish in connection with this matter. The record is clear that the Registrar who deals with these records day by day and continuously over a long period of years that he described, knew that over 50% of the registrations' records of the white voters in this parish had exactly the same kind of discrepancies that were found to be sufficient under these challenges to remove these colored voters from the registration rolls of this parish. There were 1514, colored voters registered in that parish before this action occurred. By this action, 1377 of those 1514 were removed from the rolls. There are over 11,000 white, 11,444 to be exact, on the registration rolls in this parish and only 10 of them were challenged.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Excuse me? Under this state procedure does the mere submission of the affidavit result in removal or is some determination statement?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: The Registrar has to act but as a part of proceedings, it is shown at the Registrar here, Mr. Thomas, brought an action for declaratory judgment in which he said these people were harassing, interfering with the conduct of his office and that he wanted an adjudication as to whether or not he had to make these removals from the registration rolls and whether or not it would be a violation of the injunction outstanding against him from 1950 in which he was directed by a Federal District Court not to discriminate against any voters in the registration by reason of their race or color. And in this proceeding, the state District Judge held that he -- his function was ministerial and that he had no discretion and that he was, as soon as the challenge was filed that he was to proceed to remove them from the rolls that was his duty.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: Does that mean he must remove them from the rolls or does he have a discretion, after a challenge has been initiated by these two affidavits, send out a notice, then when the voter responds to make the determination for himself as to qualifications?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Well yes, but if there was no response I think is a fair interpretation of the state judge's decision, then he had no discretion that it was his – then his duty. Now, there is an affidavit provided for in the state law, state statutes, in which they can respond by getting three voters to swear on affidavit on their behalf. This affidavit is a -- a limited form and it's provided that they are residents of the district in effect. The Court held this affidavit in the form required by the Louisiana laws will be no answer to these particular discrepancies because the -- the Court said that they would have to swear out an affidavit in which they would say that these particular discrepancies did not appear or did not occur on the record.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: Now do I understand, I mean to get this point clear in my mind, that it has been held by some court down there, that in the (Inaudible) of response by the voter, you have thus been challenged, you used the affidavits in the statutory laws, the Registrar has no discretion to overrule the challenge, but must remove the voter from the rolls.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: That's my understanding of the judgment of the state court, Mr. Justice Whittaker.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: And one more question --

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: And -- and the issue is, I might add there issue was further raised by the Registrar whether this type of discrepancy was sufficient for them to be removed from the rolls. Now the discrepancies involved here were misspelling, the miscalculation of the age and illegibility and in a few cases, there is one about whether or not he was registered in any ward. Now, it's the problem about age is where many of them missed the age by one day. The state Attorney General has said that is not a disqualification, but the state court judge said it was and the missed one day, depends upon whether you're going to calculate it by figuring in the day of the filing or you figure it out. Now, that's one and then the question of misspelling. It's a -- a very interesting exhibit attached in the Appendix to the Government's brief. To show you just how this worked in the operation and what in a front it is to the people of Louisiana, including these voters and the American people to be saying that these Negro voters were disqualified in the voting process by reason of what happened here.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: May I just -- as a matter of curiosity Mr. Solicitor, did you say that there were 15,000 whites on the voting list?

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: 11,000, just as a matter of curiosity, do you happen to know how many voted at the last election?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: No, I don't, Mr. Justice Frankfurter, I'm sorry.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: It has nothing to do with the case, I was just curious.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: Could I ask you one question and I won't interrupt you. You mentioned the 1950 injunction that had been issued against this Registrar. What Court was that issued --

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: That was the Federal District Court.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: Was there a -- is that a cited case, was there an opinion written?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: There was a -- it's set out in the record here on pages 99 and 100. The effective orders are on page 100 at the top of the page, the two paragraphs. One is summarized, restrained from denying and refusing to register eligible Negro -- Negroes as electors in this very issue. Solely, on account of raising color and the second one is an injunction against making any distinction based upon raising color and the registration of collectors in Washington Parish. That was outstanding from the date by the same district judge at that time.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: Mr. Solicitor, may I ask you one more question?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Yes, Mr. Justice.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: And I promise that would be all.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: No.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: (Inaudible) -- is there any record -- as I understand the Registrar is the only respondent (Inaudible), is that right?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Yes, because the time for appeal is not yet up for the other parties and they would have I think until March if in case they want to notice an appeal and at the time the Government's filing they had not noticed. So, the only person who had noticed an appeal was the Registrar.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: Can the Registrar be chargeable with the dissemination in – in any way in the filing of affidavit against the Negroes but not against the whites? He had anything to do that?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Well, he has the duty to administer these registration rolls and if he, as he testified he knew that over 50% of the white had exactly the same condition. He had a duty with regard to those whites as well as the colored, if he was going to administer these as being grounds for removing them from the rolls.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: That's why I was interested to know whether he had a discretion to deny the challenge? He has apparently no power to initiate a challenge against anyone.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: There's no decision of that kind and I don't think that it would be a fair conclusion from the decision of the state court. Apparently, he has the power to initiate on his own the -- if a certain person does not come in within the statutory requirement, that's his function. So that he -- he has the power, I don't think it would be disputed here to have removed this 50% of the whites if he'd proceeded to do it.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: Then -- then two affidavits (Inaudible) one challenge and not required?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Well, that is for --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap)

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: -- that can start the procedure whereby he then the Court said, has a ministerial duty to proceed to do it.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: But somebody -- is there some other way to initiate that thing?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Well, I think he has the power and duty as the Court said to carry out the statutes and he knew that this record showed that the same disabilities occurred on 50% of the whites.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: When is he making (Inaudible) about it, he might have (Inaudible) over here, the same discrepancies on Negroes and whites and affidavits were presented to him challenging the Negroes.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: But no affidavits presented to him challenging the whites. Now what can he do?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: He's not helpless because he has to take into account the Constitution of the United States and the Federal Law which is primary and supreme and controlling his action as long he's -- as you knows that the particular acts that he is act -- asked to do are in violation or discriminatory of his own knowledge then it's his duty under the law that's supreme not to carry it out.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: Alright. Then that should give him the right to overrule the challenge and refuse to remove the challenge of Negro voters, isn't it?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Well, he would have the duty not to exercise that challenge. It's the contention of the Government whenever it's discriminatory because that is prohibited by federal law and that's supreme, but I was trying to tell you what the state court had held was his duty in regard to these particular challenges ones they came to him.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: Thank you very much.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Now, if you --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: The second part of your answer to Justice Whittaker's question implied another way of answering would have been (Inaudible) decide to be more explicit, if you can disregards what you have said with the state court's ruling as to his ministerial duty, is that incorrect?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Yes Mr. Justice Frankfurter. I think you're -- you're within --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: No, I just -- I just want to know if that's what that mean?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: You're right.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That's was earlier -- you told earlier to Justice Whittaker state law so far as declared by decision of a ruling court for him, if there were no federal questions, the state also says, he has a -- he has a ministerial duty, meaning that if a formality is satisfied, he must act. What you're saying now, what -- what your answer to Justice Whittaker amount to is in the context that was you're talking about, you may disregard what is a directing decision of a state court, is that right?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: That's right, Mr. Justice Frankfurter.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Alright.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: We think it's right within In re Neagle, that's -- where the two come into clash that the can follow his federal duty and proceeding in accordance for that law. Now, if you will turn to exhibits on the Appendix 42 (a) and notice the photo stub there, you'll see that Mrs. Affoway Smith in the first one on the left hand side --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: -- of the Governments brief -- the Government -- I'm sorry, the Governments brief, page 42 (a), there's a photo stub that you unfold. You'll notice Affoway Smith and the -- the registration application in Louisiana records the fact of what's their race or color is, they're supposed to put that down. One of the things that they challenged them for and they've been removed from the rolls in -- in some of these matters for is the fact that they put down C or colored, instead of black or they put down Negro instead of black and that is the ground that they have exercised the challenge and said that doesn't comply with their statutory requirement. That isn't true of this one, but I just want to point that out as in you are examining it. Now, this woman missed the date of the note -- the time that she had lived by one day depending upon why do you include the date of the application? Now she is a teacher as you'll note from the -- the blank there in the application blank. Now, if you move down to the blank just below that, you'll find the one of Mrs. William A. Luis. She is also a teacher and she happens to be a white teacher, but she can't spell what the State of Louisiana. She's spelled it L.O.U.I.S.I.A.N.I.A., but nobody challenges her. Nobody removes her from the rolls and when she fills in the blank, the name of the householder at the present address is, that should be her own name if that's who the householder is but she fills in 4632 Avenue G, which is a improper answer to the question the way they've been running and so she should have been challenged by anybody who was trying to be non-discriminatory and moved off the rolls, but there's the white sign there and of course you don't that. Now, you go to the next page of the photostatic copy.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Was there proof in the record that Mrs. Luis is a registration had been under scrutiny?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: No. There was proof that the Registrar worked for these records all the time and knew, but there was no proof that these particular members of the citizens' counsel, white citizen's counsel that filed his challenges had examined that --

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Well, all I was wondering was the discrepancies which you point out here whether they were -- whether the record shows that they -- were might have been occasioned by overlooking each particular registrations or whether -- how these registrations were looked over and -- but not challenged.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Yes. Well, let me see if this will answer your questions Mr. Justice Harlan in this regard. I -- squarely, I don't think there -- there wasn't any proof that this particular application was looked at except that it came during the trial, but the Registrar testified, admitted that 50% of these applications in his office, he knew to have the same discrepancies as those of the colored that he removed.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: That's not accused of?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: That is -- that's admitted. So, there's no question but he was aware now but he was aware now, whether he was aware of just Affoway Smith, I -- I wouldn't say for a moment but he was aware that -- that it was all through his record as to the whites and that would be some six better than 5,000 -- 5,500 that were -- that way with the white. Now --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: Are you also aware, being aware, what can he do, the Registrar?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Oh, he has the power to correct that situation. He has difficult problems politically if he does, but he has the power to comply with these statutes, if these -- if that's what the statutes mean. I think that question is not now before the court in a way this record is. I don't think that this is a proper construction of these statutes to say that to miss the day of the time you are -- have lived by one day just because you don't whether the statutes means that you shall include the day of the application or not include it is a reasonable interpretation of this statute at all. And I don't think if you misspelled Louisiana because you add an extra "I", well that should be have any relation to your fitness to be a voter in the state of Louisiana or on the other state.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Mr. Solicitor --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) what clause did it use to (Inaudible) it disqualified as one (Inaudible) disqualified vote.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: That's what I'm saying and if the Registrar has the duty to abide these statutes, it's his function not only to act under challenges, but to act directly if these are real basic discrepancies that should control his right to be on the vote rolls.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: It is also conceded or conceded by you the color question apart, suppose there were no color question, that the Registrar could not sua sponte on his own motion throw out Mrs. Louis's registration.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Oh, I claim he could.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Apart from any question of differentiation to the whites and colored people.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Oh, I -- I say these aren't enough if that's --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Pardon me?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: -- I say this type of discrepancy should not have any bearing under the law. I don't think its constitutional to they have that kind of a measure of whether persons are qualified both.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, my --

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: I think it has no reasonable relationship with it.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I'm not sure I made my question clear.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: I'm sorry.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Suppose, there were no questions, no color discrimination there and suppose that Mrs. Luis had made -- had made an entry that in your point of view was egregious that it didn't come within the statute, color question, color discrimination apart in your view would the Registrar, could the Registrar on his own motive find her -- her registration disqualifying registration?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: In my view, Mr. Justice Frankfurter, he has that power and the duty and I think that was –

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Wholly apart from, I'm not talking on discrimination –

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: - under the Fourteenth or Fifteenth Amendment.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Now on this next page of the photo stats, Mr. James D. Cyrus misspelled Pearl River which was the county, that's on page 42 (b) of the Government's brief and you'll note here is a Negro and he was challenged and removed from the list. Then if you go there --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: What -- what was his defect?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Pearl River. He spelled, P.E.R.A.L transposed the R and the L and then if you go to the next one, Herman Manning you'll notice he -- the way he put his name in the first part there, he didn't spell it right at all. He mixed up the Herman and the Manning so it's hardly possible to decide what he meant and then the when he put down his sex, he put down “F” for female, I guess for a man. Now --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: How would the Registrar know whether he was a man or not or a female, even though they might be, unless it might -- unless it is clear, but there are number of -- of names of Marian or Adrian or Vivian that do not disclose whether male or female.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: I haven't heard of Herman not disclosed in the record.[Laughter]

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Her name. I -- I'll use that --

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: That's right there's a circle around Mr. too, Mr. Justice Frankfurter. I didn't see -- catch that.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I didn't mean as to this particular one.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: General rules or -- how do you just -- how do you (Inaudible) Herman out of the word H.E.R.M.AN.N.I.N.G.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: I'm trying to make it out in this signature Mr. Justice Frank -- Whittaker at the bottom down there. You see, his got his signature down and then he let it put the name in up above, it's different.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Who encircled the Mr.?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: I assume he did but I -- that isn't the evidence in proof.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Is -- is it been challenged by the state, if I may speak of Thomas as a state the Attorney General is here I take it --

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- is it challenged by the state that there were these numerous, identical or for all practical purposes are identical failures in spelling or in addition or substraction identification in white registrations as against colored, is that challenged here?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: No Mr. Justice Frankfurter, that's admitted in the record.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That is.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: So, apart from -- from making the case more vivid and concrete, you're -- you're not addressing yourself to something has been in issue, are you?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: No. What I'm --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I'm not -- I'm not your -- your argument in this case, and I just want to know whether there's any context on what you're now developing?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: I think it -- no, there is not to answer that.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Alright.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: I think this illuminates --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Yes --

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: -- the kind of problem that we're dealing with in this area.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I understood that that let's you know your position and I know that the counsel want to present the case to you. I just want to know whether, whether the basic, the underlying factor in dispute here or the legal consequence or merely the legal consequences and the obligation of state official etc., etc.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: No, they ‘re not.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Alright.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Are the findings challenged?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Well, I would think some of the -- the findings as to the conclusions, the Court made in regard to some of the facts as to –-

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Conclusions, the finding of the facts challenge?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Well, on some parts of that -- there -- there's no issue on the facts Mr. Justice Black. On the –

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: But there was finding, wasn't there?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Yes, there were.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, you say the findings.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Has there been any challenge to any particular part of those findings?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: They submitted findings of their own which were not the same as the Governments.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: They were not accepted?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Yes. That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Did -- did they find the points on this which they were hoping or on which they began, they appealed in the Court of Appeals? There was some doctrine indicating what they are going up on, wasn't there?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Well, it was a in --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: So the appeal is to question and determine.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: -- it was incomplete Mr. Justice Frankfurter as we say in our brief, because the notice to appeal had been filed but they had obtained a stay and so we argue points here in anticipation of positions that they might take.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Now, have they taken them in the brief?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: They have taken some in their brief.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: As to what --

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: The -- the factual situation it does not seem to be an issue between --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Does the brief challenge some other findings of facts?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Not as I recall.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: So, from the finding of fact, was Justice Black's question?

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I haven't seen them.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: My recollection is the brief does not put an issue of these facts. It puts an issue, the legal questions.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: There's one other thing before a proceedings to the legal arguments that I would like to call your attention to and that is, that by these tests which I want and I think you'll understand me but I want to make clear the Government does not think are valid constitutional tests anyway to apply these discrepancies in this manner, but they were applied in this discriminatory manner, but regardless of that, if you apply it as they did, and if you would apply them across the board to both colored and whites, this record demonstrates that you would eliminate 90% of the colored by this kind of discrepancy and 50% of the whites. Now, they didn't do that, they only challenged 10% -- 10 of the whites.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: If you're right in the statements you've just made, this irregular, type of irregular argument was not the constitutional basis upon which the Registrar may expel them from the rolls then that ends your (Inaudible), doesn't it?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Yes, if you would conclude that. Now that was not –

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: I didn't say, I said assume we did --

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: That was not the basis of the decision below and you might not -- and you wouldn't reach that question if you found a discrimination contrary to the Constitution which was the basis of the lower court.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: If we found it?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Well, if you found that he was, it is judgment to that effect was valid.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: The trial court's finding (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: The trial courts finding--

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: For it's evidence.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: That's right and they were fully supportive and much of it, there was no controversy about it at all.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: In considering it do we have before us a permanent injunction or an interlocutory injunction?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: You have the permanent injunction.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: The permanent injunction.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Yes Mr. Justice.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I'm a little (Inaudible) minute or two or two minutes, in some of the seven states surely, there are all white counties (Inaudible) somewhere in all black county.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: That is true.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Now, I don't know whether that's in Louisiana or not, but assume an all white county in Louisiana, assume that, and the rejection for the striking from the rolls was made on what has been nominated several times as unconstitutional criteria, a misspelling of -- of one's own name by -- or things like that or being wrong as this is (Inaudible) by a day. Assume an white county, no discrimination but white vote was struck, was stricken, names of the white vote was stricken from the list. Would there be any -- is there any constitutional – federal, is there any -- what is the law -- what -- on what basis if the Government or anybody else go in challenge state law and say, “You deprive me of my right to vote on this trivial misspelling,” is there?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Wouldn't the -- this test that the state has to apply about voting in regard to -- certainly in regard to federal elections have to be reasonably rate -- related to that franchise or that voting. That's what I'm trying to point out and if --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And you'll have to -- it would be a very the problem, I must say in this one.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: The state might be very strict in its demand for literacy. As of the present Congress could, tomorrow this afternoon if it shows, makes its own specific requirements as to federal -- as to the federal franchise, but until it has done so, the fact that the state is very strict and demands a high rate of literacy (Inaudible), I should think you would have a very different case that if you came here on that Mr. Solicitor?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Well, that is a different problem. I do think it's important to -- in regard to literacy as the Court did in the Lastner case to keep in mind that if your literacy test is one that obviously is going to discriminate --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That's what I do solely, yes.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: -- on the basis of raise or color, then it's one that can't stand up against the --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And what you answered a little while ago, I think to Justice Whittaker, if the same test of a -- of a failure to live up or a very fastidious standard would throw out the 10% of whites and 50% of Negroes, you also got a different problem there.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: It's very --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I was thinking of -- of the intrinsic invalidity of a very high in my point of view, even exquisite standard of literacy or intelligence. If it is (Inaudible) done, I think one would of the other tough job saying that's the deposition of your federal franchise.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: As long as it's really related to literacy, I wouldn't have any real problem about it.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I thought your entire argument was based on the fact it is discriminating here on the basis of color?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: That's right Mr. Justice Black.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: That case is based in the findings?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: I'm a little confused to what you said to Justice Black. I thought this was a preliminary injunction as it's described in your brief rather than a permanent one, but if you look at the injunction, it has all the earmarks of being permanent, which is the fact?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: I thought that it was a preliminary injunction originally and then they granted permanent.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Under the --

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: (Inaudible) there's a temporary injunction.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: It's still a preliminary injunction.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: From your point of view, I should think you'd like to have a temporary injunction.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: I don't see how you read it between those temporary. However, as well as the record 153 and 154, (Inaudible) the Court is called on to take a further step, I don't think you (Inaudible)

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Well, I inquired about that from the man who tried the case and he thought it was a permanent injunction. So, I think possibly it's the same difficulty that you Justices are having with it that it was a denominated preliminary, but in a form it was quite permanent.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: With this very jurisdiction?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Yes. The reason I have dwelled upon this proposition of the discrepancy and the effect of this -- even this type of discrepancies or -- or defects in these applications was that this case presents to the Court the problem even if it has been administered fairly and without discrimination as far as applying the same test to the white as to the black, you would have a discrimination in effect because you would eliminate 50% by this test of the white as against 90% of the black.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Then you get back to as I understand your problem which is you have finding, saying this was done on the acaccount of color and -- and there is no challenge to it, but as I understand those findings that I see, that as I understand it you open this evidence to show that they're supported -- argument showed that they're supported by evidence? Do they claim they're not supported by evidence?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Well, we anticipated that they might make that claim in the way this case came up you see, because we didn't -- we weren't able to tell when the Court took case by -- at the time we filed out petitions and also our brief what their contentions were going to be. So, we argued that as well as the rest and we have to do that because --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Did they challenge the findings in the Court after they were made to the District Court?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: I don't recall if they did Mr. Justice Black, but we did argue that they were fully supported by the evidence because the Court in taking the case here, although we won it below the Government did, the Court asked us if we wanted to -- to file a petition by a certain date. So, although we have been the successful party below, we filed the petition for certiorari here and then we briefed it first without knowing fully what the other side was going to develop against us. And so, we tried to anticipate all the arguments that would be open to the parties if they came and they tried to show that the findings in fact -- of fact and conclusions of law.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Did they file one brief or two?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: They filed one.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: This brief?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Yes. And that was filed on Saturday, this last Saturday by the Court's order and we -- we examined it. I'm not complaining about that at all, but I just wanted to show you how it lead us to the point where we had to develop our case and try to anticipate arguments as we did. And so, we tried to show that the findings were fully justified and in much of the evidence it was admitted. Now, to proceed to the question of law, we get to the question of whether this is state action.

turns.name: JUSTICE DOUGLAS
turns.text: Why -- why is that important?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Whether it's state action? Well, the contention is made like in Raines that --

turns.name: JUSTICE DOUGLAS
turns.text: In the -- you have a statute here that says -- subsection (b) in page 3 whether acting on the appellant law or other way.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Under subsection (b). We are treating subsection (a) and (c).

turns.name: JUSTICE DOUGLAS
turns.text: Well, (c) says that violation of (b) gives the Attorney General the right to institute an action. If (b) is involved, well, I'm reading (c).

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I am wondering also if it wouldn't come under 80 U.S. Code, 241 conspiracy no more question for anything (Inaudible) so forth and it doesn't pre exercise the enjoyment of the right which been held Constitution.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Mr. Justice Black --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- and most of the case.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: This case was brought by the Attorney General and it was under the power of this statute of the 1957 Civil Rights Act --

turns.name: JUSTICE DOUGLAS
turns.text: Under the power you -- you brought it under that statute. Does that tie you down to any particular statute as far as the violation of law is concerned and conspiracy is concerned?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Well, it's conceived that the Attorney General's own action could be brought in -- under this particular statute for this type of violation. Now, the conspiracy under 241 was a different problem as far as the Attorney General brings the action himself. The -- there's no question but what individuals could bring certain actions under the civil rights statutes before them. And Section (b) of this statute has been construed by the Government to cover the intimidation and the threatening and coercion or attempt to intimidate, threaten or coerce any other person, Mr. Justice Douglas, rather than dealing with this type of preventing or denying the right to vote. See, it's -- deals with the intimidation and threatening and coerce or attempt for the process of interfering and so forth. Now, we -- we treated this as being under subsection (a) and that the action could be brought under subsection (b) because it was not an intimidation or threatening. It was actually the using of Governmental process as of the state to accomplish this discrimination and it was a state action because they -- they took advantage of what the statute provided for these affidavit challenges and then they submitted them, they swore to them and used the office of the Registrar in order to get all the information. They swore to --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Who -- who used them?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Well, they were these persons that swore out the affidavit who were members of --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: They were not the state officers, were they?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: No. They were members of the white citizens' council. They swore out the affidavits that are provided for under this state statute and put into -- they triggered, initiated the state action and then that Registrar proceeded to act in accordance with their challenges, but they were the ones that triggered that state action and the statute itself contemplates that private individuals shall do that.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: If that's so, why do you talk about the state action (Inaudible) these private individuals?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Well, because it's against --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: What do you have to do it?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: It's a -- it's a panoply. It's a combination.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, I don't know what panoply means.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: It's -- it's a --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I understand when you say that a person is not a state officer and the state is not to do it, but I don't quite understand your panoply.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Well, maybe I can clarify Mr. Justice Black that the persons who filed the affidavits started the action, but the --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Private --private person.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: -- but they can't remove them from their rolls. The Registrar has to do that.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Now, what you're saying in -- isn't it that if a private persons joined with somebody else to take away these rights, these private persons are allowed whether they are acting for the state or not.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Yes, Mr. Justice Black.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: That's what I thought.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: The -- the only problem we have about a conspiracy is that the -- the evidence did not show that the Registrar conspired with these people. He claimed they were interfering in this lawsuit with his operation and conduct of his office and therefore, that he want them to stop. That's what he said anyway in the litigation. And yet, he proceeded to do all of the things that they wanted to do and when they filed only 10 affidavits in regard to whites in the parish, he didn't say, “Well, what about all these others that I know about?” or, he didn't proceed to remove any of those, but he proceeded to carry out the discrimination that they had started.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: Well, Mr. Solicitor General, I -- I'm just still concerned about that question. Now, let's take Section 244 of record -- at the Louisiana's brief, 122. Now, let me -- this says, “Whenever the Registrar has reason to believe that any name on the registration record has been illegally or fraudulently placed there then he may ask,” on the next Section of -- at this brief, page 125, Section 245 says, “Upon a written affidavit signed and sworn to unduplicated before and filed with the Registrar his deputy by two bona fide voter -- registered voters, he must act.” Now, suppose that the Registrar under Section 244 does not believe these irregularities make the registration illegal, he therefore doesn't ask.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Well, he --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: But -- but under -- but -- but under 245, affidavits are filed, and then you tell us that he must act and that he has no discretion even to find against the challenge. Is that what you want us to understand?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Well, if you're going to apply the state law and disregard his federal duty, we say that the federal duty doesn't permit him to act as either one. But brushing that aside, the state court said that these were persons with these discrepancies were illegally on the rolls. So, he comes right within their Section 244 because he testified, he admitted it, that he knew that 50% of them have those and he handled these records everyday. So, he knew that 50% were illegally on the rolls of the white persons and it was his duty under that section of the statute, if you disregard his federal duty not to discriminate to remove them. It's clear. Now, he said that he didn't think these were enough and so he went to the state court and asked and the state court said that it was enough. The power is certainly granted to him by that Section.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: Well, only if he believes that he has reason to believe, then he being convinced may act, but under 245 that the affidavits he'd filed, he must act.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: Now then, is it true that he has no discretion under 245 to sustain the registration and overrule the challenge?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Well, the -- the court held -- the state court held that he had no discretion that it was a ministerial act.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: But if that violated the supreme federal rights, but of course he did (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: And that's --

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: And that's what we claim was his legal duty and it overrides and it was the supreme duty and therefore, he had no right to do it. We say he had no right to do it, because it was discriminatory.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: That was added up for me.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: But --

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: I was going to say as far as we're concerned, the defendant's duty, the Registrar's duty as measured by the state supreme -- by the state law is what this Court said it was his duty, that's how we have to take the law.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Yes. As far as the (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: As a state law duty

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: -- state court --

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: -- Duty.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: That's right. Then I think you put a --

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: But we don't have to worry --

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: -- clause on that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: We don't have to worry about how we read the statute.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Well, that -- that's my interpretation Mr. Justice Harlan and then I think you put gloss of the federal law as to determine what's supreme.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: I'm talking about the state law.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Yes. And he had the power according to the state court's interpretation to act on his own under the first section and -- well, the Court told him, "These were enough" so he had no discretion Mr. Justice Whittaker once he was told that he by -- by the Court that this was enough as far as the state court law was concerned, that section -- the statute gave him the power to act --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: But --

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: -- and had duty to that.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: Duty?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Yes, because they're illegal according to the state court decision and there you get into the question of the discrimination between the two. Now, the further question was raised in regard to the type of relief that was granted, whether or not it was justified under the circumstances, and the findings of the fact of the Court and we think it was amply justified. The legislative history shows that it was contemplated by the Congress at the time the 1957 Civil Rights Act was passed that this very type of problem was discussed by the Attorney General at that time in presenting it before the Senate Judiciary Committee, the Subcommittee on constitutional rights and also before the House Judiciary Committee that this very example was discussed of this case, but an -- a case is similar to it in regard to the persons being removed from the registration rolls and how it would be handled and the Senators, Senator Morris and Senator Kero discussed specifically the idea of their being reinstated upon the rolls so they'd be able to vote and it was also discussed by Congressman Miller in the House Judiciary Committee that very thing and the conduct of removing the registration rolls shortly before election in Louisiana it was gone into at length and finally, Senator Douglas asked that he be supplied some information by (Inaudible) only the third in charge of the criminal division, regard to four different, five different parishes that were involved in removing these Negroes from the rolls in Louisiana in 1956. And he furnished that information in detail and it's set out in the appendix to our brief showing how they worked with this to get them off of the roll and Senator Douglas made that a part of his remarks when he addressed the senate in regard to this Bill and some other of this pretty shameful story. It tells how in some places, the Registrar, that isn't this case, but the Registrar would not allow more than 50 attempts during the day to get their names – the Negroes to get their names back on the registration rolls. They would come there at 5 o'clock in the morning, stay all day and when 50 would be completed then they wouldn't have anymore and that went on for days in this one parish and they weren't able to get many of those names back on the rolls.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Now assuming -- assuming invalid or unconstitutional action, what cause or remedies are suggested in supposing what was done here by Judge Wright?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Well, there is the suggestion that they tried to get reregister and that is not an easy process in Louisiana. On page --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: You mean -- you mean go to Court?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: No, there -- there is a provision on the constitution for going to Court, but their Court has divided on whether that is proper or exclusive way and that is referred to in our -- set in the -- in the record here and also in briefs referred to, but there's also a remedy by just trying to reregister again and doing it correctly, but then you'd have to pass these tests.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: You mean go the same Registrar --

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- with a (Inaudible) answer -- with (Inaudible) answer.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Well, then you go to pass tests on -- on the questions that set out on page 38 at the bottom of the page and you've got to be a good character and you've to understand the duties and obligations of citizenship under republican form of Government.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: You mean there are no -- no --

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: New test.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: New test.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Because you failed the first one.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: That's right. All you got this where that these discrepancies were there and then you're liable for a perjury under their statute if you swear anything like that. Well, you couldn't swear to that because it would be a false and sometimes this -- this legislative history shows in 1956, they would get some white persons to swear off to the statutory affidavit for the colored persons. And the Registrar says, "Oh, you can't have that, you got to have colored persons" and when they get to colored persons, they say, "Oh, you got to have an officer to say that you're -- you'd -- in this -- in this residence (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: These rulings by the Registrar, are they defined by the statute if they got to have a -- a colored -- potential colored voter must have colored --

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: No.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- supporters?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: No, those are just --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That's his ruling?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Those are his rulings and some -- now there was a case that was described by Mr. Alaman to the Senate Committee in which the Registrar was decent and fair about the whole thing and when they made the showing, he put their names back on the rolls. That's one out of the five, but the rest of it was this history of all kinds of arbitrary things. Now, there's also furnished as a part of the legislative history to Senator Douglas that's set out in the appendix a copy of a newspaper account of the fact that white citizen's counsel at in around 1956 or thereabouts decided upon this kind of course of action and trying to see how they – many Negroes, they could get off the registration list in this -- in Louisiana and it's described in detail and it's made part of the record by Senator Douglas when he obtained it from Mr. Alaman. And the -- the idea was that even if they had remove some whites, they get as many colored off as possible, they just said so in these to counsel there meetings and so forth. Now, on this particular action, this action of these particular parties that challenged these colored voters on this registration list was approved and endorsed by the white citizen's council of this parish. And it's a part of that general action.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Mr. Solicitor General, it's very (Inaudible) -- your own kind of argument but I'm more than a little disturbed personally about the -- the posture of this case. It is true, is it not, that except from the Registrar and except for Mr. Tomas, the Registrar and not on the other parties that -- defendants are here.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: That's right Mr. Justice.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Their time to appeal to the United States Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit has not yet expired.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Insofar, they haven't filed those appeals, I guess, have they?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: I haven't checked --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: As far as we know.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: -- but don't know.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Certiorari of course is not being granted in this case yet the petition is pending, that's true, isn't it?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: That's right, but you must keep in mind April 19th of the election date too.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Well there is also of course a motion here is set aside to stay vacated?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: -- stay, is there not?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Yes. Now, the problem, there's one -- first thing to keep in mind in that -- regard to that entire problem however, that is a discriminatory action that we're talking about in violation of the Constitution and if the discrimination had not occurred, these people along with the whites would be right on that voting list and have the opportunity to vote just like all of these whites. There's no question throughout this other people just whites having a right to vote regardless of a discrepancies because that's the way it's operates, no issue about it. So that the discriminatory action is prohibited by the constitution that we're trying to reach and that the Congress was trying to reach too in passing this act, it will just this very business of doing it at a time when it would be difficult for the Courts to act properly enough to get it corrected and therefore, you got the job done regardless.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: What kind of election is this going to be on the April 19th?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Well, it's their second election, their run off election.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: It's their general election.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: General election.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: (Voice Overlap)

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: State general election.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: State general election.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: They have had the –

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: -- and which this -- now only one candidate for Democratic party and the other one is in (Inaudible)

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: I think there's --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: -- for Governor (Inaudible) Democratic-Republican nominees?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: It's one of these five --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: States right.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: -- states right candidate on the other side.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: What effect is there (Inaudible)

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: It would might be difficult if we --

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: We're worried about the (Inaudible)

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Oh, we're worried about both Mr. Justice Clark because I don't think these votes can be evaluated just how valuable they are and they even though maybe the -- the best votes for a man to have in Louisiana is to win the primary because he has more voice in the result if he does nevertheless, I think if you can -- if it can be done by discrimination they can keep race or color from voting then just by accomplishing it doesn't make any difference how it works out as long as they get it done and to defeat it, you have to try to beat that particular day if possible, that's our problem.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Mr. Solicitor, may I ask you to turn to the decree of the Court because after all that -- that's before us on page 163 of the record and when you have it, I'd like to put a question to you if I may.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Assume -- assume that the decree only subjected or that the decree only governed or controlled the action as to taken or not to be taken, the (Inaudible) with paragraph 3 maybe in (c) of 3 and paragraph 4, paragraph 1 and 2 implicates other people and paragraph 5 my hasty reading of the (Inaudible) also may raise an question, but three and four, the record exclusively (Inaudible) Thomas. Am I right about that?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Yes Mr. Justice.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Now, suppose the decree only was to carry out paragraphs 3 (a) and (b), and (c), and four, would that abort what was sought by this suit? Would that intimate what you're asking? If you just had that to be enforced and if that was respected for Curtis and Thomas, which I assume you would -- would obey (Inaudible) told you ultimately what Judge Wright's decree, would that effectuate, what the suit is about?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Mr. Justice, I think it would. The only problem left would be whether or not they tried to get additional challenges out and I would think that under this, the Registrar wouldn't recognize any additional challenges either wouldn't dare to.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, in the first place, this 1377 are now -- those would be dealt, wouldn't they?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: As to the future, there might hypothetically be raised the question that you raise, but I should doubt it in obedience for the decree. I mean people would -- any good lawyer would say you're awful chance --

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That's -- that right as far as contempt if you disobey this.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Well, there are only 200 left anyway I think.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Yes Mr. Justice Harlan. But I thought Mr. Justice Frankfurter is (Inaudible) Well, what I was trying to deal with the possibility that there could be challenges again to this --

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: All of these people.

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: And in the light of the state court decree that he would still have to obey them at all, but I think that's a very remote --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: It (Inaudible) his agent and his successor?

turns.name: J. LEE RANKIN
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: So that --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Solicitor General, you may have 10 minutes to -- additional to respond and the counsel may have 10 minutes additional also if you wish upon your time. Mr. Cousins -- do I -- do I -- pronounce it correctly?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Cousins. That's correct. Oh, Cousins.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: I misunderstood the Attorney General.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Attorney General. Mr. Chief Justice and the associate justices, this action -- I would like to clear up a few facts as to how this matter appears before this Court. The original complaint was filed by the United States Government here under the part IV of the Civil Rights Act of 19 -- the Civil Rights Act of 1957, 42 U.S.C. 1971 and particularly Section A thereof. The original complaint asked for a permanent injunction, motion to dismiss was filed by the Attorney General on behalf of the Registrar of Voters and by the other defendants. After the motion to dismissed was denied, the Government --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: The Attorney General appear for the other defendants?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: No, he did not if it pleases the Court. I think the Court is familiar with the fact that the Attorney General just recently by an act of a new statute been appointed as the counsel for the Registrar of Voters wherein he is involved in federal litigation in courts for state's rights.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But not the -- not the evidence.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: The other individuals -- the Registrar had an individual counsel which does not appear in this matter today and the individual citizens who made the initial challenges where represented by their individual counsel. None of whom may have taken an appeal herein, the Registrar being the only one before the Court today. After the motion to dismissed and was denied, the Government filed and a motion for a preliminary injunction, various testimony was taken and the Government file as to leave to the Court to make copies of some 250 registration cards which were filed in the record on a hearing. This matter was merely presented on the evidence of affidavits et cetera and so forth there was absolutely in no testimony other than one deposition taken of the Registrar of Voters. So, it's purely a preliminary injunction matter with no testimony involved. The decree on the preliminary injunction was the decree from which the Attorney General filed a motion to stay in the Circuit Court of Appeals pending the perfection of the appeal. The day the -- the appeal -- the state ought it was granted by the Circuit Court, 24 hours later, this Court was furnished with a motion by the United States by the Solicitor General for the hearing which is here today and of course you aren't aware of the decree which you rendered therein.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: You construe the decree as being only an interlocutory one then?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: On question, yes, if it pleases the Court. Now, its context may rely on those factors for the purposes of its form, it is to us an interlocutory decree of -- interlocutory decree. Now, this Civil Rights Act under which the -- the action is brought here find it's origin in the Fifteenth Amendment. Of course, I'm sure -- if it apprises this Court of the wording of the Fifteenth Amendment wherein it merely denies the -- the United States or any of the states from abridging the rights of persons to vote because of their color.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Is that all it does?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: I beg your pardon? If you please --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Is that all the Fifteenth Amendment does?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Of course, we are --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I'm talking about the language of --

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: No. If it please --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I understood you say that's what it did.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: No. I'm sure you that you're aware of the fact of the negative nature of the Fifteenth Amendment wherein --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I -- I mean the last clause of it.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Where, Section 2?

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: What is the last clause of it?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: I'm sorry?

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: What is the last clause of the Fifteenth Amendment?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Whereby Congress may pass all laws -- and legislation in the (Inaudible) of these powers?

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: I am aware of that. Also of course are contention here is particularly that this matter which lends to the constitutionality of it, well, we would like to discuss later, if it please the Court right now, we're speaking of the jurisdiction, the jurisdictional clause of this whole matter wherein the Civil Rights Act comes under the Fifteenth Amendment which gives that negative power which creates a negative power to forbid the deprivation of right because of the race and color or creed. Now, the Civil Rights Act under which this is brought specifically state that the matter -- the subsection indicates that all citizens of the United States who are otherwise qualified by law that's Subsection 8 of this matter. We have here a question of jurisdiction based upon the fact our contention being that these persons are not otherwise qualified by law. This Court, I'm sure is a (Inaudible) very many decisions wherein it's been said that the question of qualifications of voters is strictly within the states itself and this -- this matter incidentally does concern state elections. The election is coming up on the 9th -- April 19th, 1960 where it seems that this necessity for reinstating these people back on the road is a state election and no federal official being involved in this particular election.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But I see if I understand you Mr. Attorney General, otherwise -- well, otherwise qualified, it can't mean can it that the state court may disqualify them and therefore, there's a state determine this qualification and therefore they're not citizens or otherwise qualified.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: I -- I was saying --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Otherwise, that would really -- could notify obviously very simply, federal law.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Well, I -- I do not think it -- I would carry my contention that far if it please the Court, but I would think that a question of qualification the -- it's merely the contention of the United States, the complainant herein, that these persons are qualified. The question of whether or not they are qualified I think should be passed upon by a state court and rightfully so, the qualification of voters being within the state to determine --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, but the very -- the very issue of whether or not, this is all argumentative (Inaudible) is this --

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Yes. I understand.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: If the claim is that the disqualification itself is a violation of federal law, then that can't be determined by the state court so as to prevent it being examined in the federal court that to be -- has to be Attorney General under 1957 Act, would it?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Well, we are --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Is that your argument?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: My argument is -- is that part, portion of it, a question is develop later when we get to the point or whether or not these people were qualified so as to come under this statute by the facts in the matter. Of course, I will go into that point at this moment if you wish please.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I thought you would. I think that this mean that they must be 21 rather than 19 contentions –

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: I understand --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- to think like that. That there is not, in other words, were otherwise meaning on matters not in dispute to entitle and to vote, but as to the matter that is in dispute, on the claim of federal supremacy it doesn't mean to see how that is to be state determined.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: I would say to answer to Mr. -- Mr. Justice, that the question of whether or not the clause are qualified is a fact governed by the -- the literacy test as imposed by the state. Of course here, we are dealing with a question of fact whether or not the discrepancy of a day or a misspelling of the words and so forth is enough to disqualify this person as a voter merely because they were on the registration rolls does not necessarily mean that they are qualified --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap)

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: For this reason they may have left their names on the rolls and move to a different parish, they might have died, they might have been felons and lost their right to vote because of that.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I follow that.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: And likewise, I would like to mention to the Court that this mere challenging, the mere issuance of a challenge, the affidavit of challenge does not disqualify a person in Louisiana from voting. And that I think is a basic issue here which the contention of our argument that this Court lack jurisdiction because this suit is brought on a basis of challenges which were issued to these various points. When the affidavit of challenge is made in the State of Louisiana, the -- the Registrar of Voter -- Voters as mentioned as much my Solicitor General has no discretion in the matter. He must as a mandatory ministerial act accept the said challenge. The person who is challenged receives from him within the -- within two days, a citation in which citation is stated the cause of challenge, the cause of challenge is sent to this man. He has 10 days after receiving the citation to come in and attempt to traverse the challenge made. If he does successfully do so, he remains on the rose as a Registrar of Voter and he then yet has a right to vote. If he self-excludes himself by not answering the challenge within the 10 days, and I say to this Court that he is the man who had denied himself of the right to vote and not the man who filed the affidavit. He had disqualified himself from voting by not having answered the challenge. Again, at the end of the 10 days, if he has not answered this challenge, the Registrar of Voters again has a mandatory duty, not a discretionary duty, a mandatory duty to expunge him from the rose of registered voters.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: May I ask you this --

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- in this connection? I -- I'm interested in your discussion. It seems to me like your problem here is this. The supreme law as you said that you cannot do this on the account of color. You had a finding that it was done on the acaccount of color. So, you don't claim that the state can bar him on the acaccount of color, these people on a acaccount of color --

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: We said the state could not bar him on a account of color.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: It cannot be.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: If you can get down, do you not to the question of whether the finding was right in this case.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: That's correct. I would get to the point that the -- whether he was qualified or not is the real issue, not his color. There is no --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: But, but not whether he's qualified, but whether he's -- whether you have put him out of voting because of his color.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: That is the issue. That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I don't see were all these others have much to do with it because the sole issue here, have they been put off because of color.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Well, I would say the sole issue is does he have a right to come into the federal courts claiming abridgment of a constitutional right. Let's assume for argument sake that one did -- an individual citizen did want to -- did want to challenge him because of his color. The mere fact that this individual challenged him would not give the United States a cause of action against that individual. Maybe the individual might have been discriminatory in his -- in his theory or in his motivating cause for challenging.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: What difference does it make about why it was done? The issue here is, under the findings that I see, solely whether these people have been barred from voting in the State of Louisiana on a account of color.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Well, I say by the state, if it please the Court.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, if the Registrar did it, that's the state, isn't it?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Yes, I would say that the Registrar had the mandatory duty but he did not --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, he don't have a mandatory --

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: He did not discriminate.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: He doesn't have a mandatory duty, does it, if -- if it's on the account of color?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Well, he has --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Can the state allow him to say that it's mandatory on you to remove this man because of color?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: I think not.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, do you do not file to get back to the one issue that you have to meet? I don't see you can meet it. Have they -- does the record show that these people, the findings that were made that there were barred on account of color is supported by evidence?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: I think not, Your Honor. It's absolutely --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: That's (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: -- no evidence to that -- to that effect. Each challenge issued has its various defects which lend to the literacy and to the qualification of these parties which would lend to a fact as to whether or not this Court has jurisdiction, to whether or not they're entitled to come in or the United States Attorney General is entitled to come under the Civil Rights Act under -- under Section C which gives him that entitlement when interstate action barring a party from discriminating against his people solely on because of race, color, or previous condition of servitude. To get to the --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But you need by ask some questions in order to bring out the fact of the whole electoral process down there that is as I understand it. As I understand from the decree of Judge Rice 3 (b) he says there, he must permit the names to remain all for present and current roles or qualified voters. That means they're off the rolls.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Now, what is the relation between being off the rolls and having the right to vote? Can you vote if you're off the rolls?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: You may not.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: You may not. After they've been put off the rolls which is indispensable to the exercise to the franchise.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And as Justice Black has indicated, the -- the findings of facts are that they are off the roll because they were taken off and they were taken off because of they were colored.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: I do not agree with the statement but --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: No, isn't that Judge Wright found?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Judge Wright found we claim that the findings of fact or in error for the reason that the -- they were not taking-- taken off the rolls because they were colored, they were taken off the rolls because they did not respond to the challenges issued.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Yes and the -- and the -- they didn't respond to the challenges issued because from their point of view, a colored man who did this doesn't -- doesn't qualify whereas a white man does and that's what I call discrimination if it's true.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: I think that it's not true if it please the Court --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, then you are really addressing yourself. You don't stop as Justice Black started in this question, namely there were findings of the facts that they were taken off the rolls, because they were colored people although the reason given was, that they didn't spell the name right or put an extra I or didn't cross the thing, but you say that you don't accept those findings as the starting point for legal discussion.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: That's correct, I do not.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: I said the basis -- the basis of the challenges issued herein were because of the lack of the failure of these parties to pass intelligence test, the -- in the literacy test as prescribed by the state laws for qualification. I should like to show -- read to you a couple of the applications keeping in mind that these applications were photostated by and at the request of United States Government. They form a part of this record. However, by stipulation between counsel, they have not been printed in the record but these are the identical copies which were furnished to us by the Attorney General -- the United States Attorney General's office. We have here one from Miss Mamie Myles. Of course, she spells Louisiana in outlandish way also, L-O-U-I-S-I-A-N-A.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: I beg your pardon?

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: How does she spell it, I'm curious.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: L-O-U-I-S-I-A-N-N-A-S, she has Washington spelled, W-A-S-G-T-O-N in answers the question, I have recite in the state since “all my lift “ L-I-F-T. In the parish continuously all lift, L-I-F-T. My -- my color is black, my -- my sex is female, F-E-L-M-E-L. I now, affiliate with the Democratic party D-E-N-C-R-A-T-T party.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: Mr. -- Mr. Cousins, I'm interested to know, could the Registrar as matter of right have refused to sustain challenges on those particular persons, to those particular reasons?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
