turns.text: If he followed the Louisiana law, he may not incidentally. I think it's important to the --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: He has a description doesn't he?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Yes, and he may not -- he may not --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: He may not? I thought all the Registrars in the southern states have a wide discretion.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: To refuse a challenge?

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: To -- to refuse --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Or the man can vote. I'm not --

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: I'm sorry Mr. Justice Black. I think Mr. Justice Whittaker's --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: We're talking about the same thing -- we are talking about the same thing --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: Same thing to reject to -- to overrule the challenge even though these spelling errors appear may been presented by the affidavit signed by two voters challenging the voter, the voter comes in and he says, “Yes, I admit that I made these spelling errors,” does the Registrars do have power to leave him on the rolls?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: I would say he has a duty to remove him if this -- if the --if the -- have power affidavits to traverse the challenge do not successfully traverse the challenge. However --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: it can't -- he can't by counter affidavits correct the spelling on the registration card --

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Because the Registrar of Voters could have an idea that the -- this word spelled the way that the Registrar had spelled it. In which case, of course the challenged party would take the Registrar to quote or to Court on their mandamus and have the local court -- the state court decide who was correct, the Registrar and the registrant or the challengers.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But he knew how to spell Louisiana if your Registrar in -- in one of these counsels on these parishes himself spells it correctly and he looks at the --at the -- he looks at the registration and see that was spelled incorrectly, it has extra N now what not. Do I understand you when you say that that's sustained of challenge because automatically Louisiana is misspelled and it was challenged because he misspelled and therefore you'll remove them from the roll, is that your view?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Well, Your Honor if it please, I think you would be violating his own knowledge about the matter. Surely, he could do it in confines of his office but his office requires him to let only registered voters on the rolls. He may even --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) -- and even in his spelling, you said -- I'm asking I am not --

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: If he -- does it challenged because of a misspelling. Louisiana is misspelled as we've seen it and if -- if the challenge is to the correctness of the spelling and the Registrar in good conscience and simply reading says, yes it is misspelled. Is he under duty to sustain that challenge?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: I would say he would be under duty -- to sustain that challenge.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Suppose he knew the man, known him many years and he was a fine, high class, good citizen, wasn't well educated but he's a good citizen, everybody liked him. You say that he would have to remove him from the roll, because he's misspelled the words?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Your Honor, I would say that if he had not --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Would you say that?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Yes, I would if he has not (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Under the law of Louisiana?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: I beg pardon?

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Under the law of Louisiana?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: If he was challenged.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: If he was challenge because he misspelled one word.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: I would say that he has not met the literacy test. Now, of course we do have a system of where illiterates may vote under Louisiana law where they don't have to fill out these cards.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: What about this gentleman named Mr. Herman somebody?

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: He's a man I am told.[Laughter]

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: I beg pardon?

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Mr. Herman is a man, did you look at his? Did -- was it right to let him vote?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Yes. If Your Honor please, that would be under the discretion of the Registrar --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Why?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: -- whether or not he want --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Why would it be in the discretion?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Whether he wanted to file -- there is no -- I agree with you that there is no definite standards for that as far as I know is to--

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: That is right. You submit, it's always this way I think I know it.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: It's the law which gave the Registrar the very wide broad discretion looking at the man, looking at his education and knowing his -- his standing in the community and so forth to take him or not him as he saw fit.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: You Honor, if may it please, I think if he does that he is not doing his duty under the law. I think there are certain standards have been set by various decisions of the courts as to whether or not --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, one other answer is that he must not misspell a single word.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: If Your Honor please I don't --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: I didn't, I said there are many standards which have been set by the other Court.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Suppose you misspelled six words, he's a good citizen, everybody knows it, a good man in the community, high standard, he's a reputable man, is it your idea that a Registrar has to remove him from the roll?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: I would say that would be strictly within the prerogative of the Registrar whether or not he is going to carry out his duty if it may please the Court.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: -- prerogatives, you mean discretion or duty?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: I would say he has a duty to see whether or not a person who registers has met the minimum literacy test of the state.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: You're speaking under Section 244 now, that's under Section 244?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: 244, I think it's a kind of challenge.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: All right, now, let's go to 245 --

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: -- if it please the Court.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: -- which is Mr. Justice Black situation where the man is presented the affidavit, two affidavits have been presented against him, saying he misspelled the word -- the word Louisiana, he has 10 days within which to rejoin, doesn't he?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: He comes in but he cannot correct the misspelling of Louisiana in the original registration challenge.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: Now then, answering Mr. Justice Black's question quite specifically has the Registrar any power to leave him on the roll or must he strike him off?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: I thought I answer that before by saying he has the power to leave on the rolls. However again, the challenger has the power to take him into Court and let the Court determine whether or not this applicant has met the standards.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- you -- I thought I understood you -- I thought I understood you when you said that if a challenge is made to a fellow who is on the rolls, on the ground that he doesn't need a literacy test and the Registrar finds that in fact, the -- the challenged voter has misspelled. I thought I understood you but you said, if he's performing his duty he must accept that challenge as sustained. Did I misunderstand?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Your Honor, of course, you're placing me in the position here of trying to determine what each Registrar around the State of Louisiana would do --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: No, I'm asking you what your conception is under the law of the duty of the Registrar not whether he wants to wink an eye but -- but does he under the law may do what Judge -- Justice Black suggested? The fellow has made eight misspellings but the Registrar knows him, he make a good citizen and he's an honorable man and all the rest, has he -- that much leeway to decide whether it comes within the statute.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Your Honor here has no more leeway than the courts would allow him if he allowed this --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But if you're right about that, then it's repeating the thing over -- did I understood you a little while ago?

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, Mr. Cousins, may I -- may I put it in just little -- little different way. If the challenge is made, is it anything more than a mere ministerial duty on the part of the Registrar to remove it?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: He -- if that --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Is it a ministerial duty of his to then remove it?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: The only time when a minimum amount of discretion would come in on the part of the Registrar is the situation as Justice Frankfurter and Justice Whittaker have mentioned that when an attempt is made to traverse a challenge made then, there would be a certain play of discretion on the part of the Registrar. However, that -- that discretion of his would again be controlled by a decision of the court in case this -- in the effect, either the challenger or the challenge would take him to test into the state courts. His only discretion would lie as again I repeat when affidavits to traverse the challenge are brought in. Other than that, he has no discretion. He must accept the affidavits. If the parties do not come in within the 10 days prescribed, he must mandatorily expunge these people from the registration rolls.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: Well the, but -- but how -- please tell me I don't understand how would I go about traversing by an affidavit, a misspelled word in the registration certificate?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Your Honor if it please, it's -- it's conceivable that the person who made the challenge was in error in making the challenge on the misspelled word. Again like the age here is quite conceivable at the person who challenged on the basis of age was wrong in the challenge, and if a party who was challenged could bring an affidavit to prove that they were correct and the challenger was in error, then certainly they'd be entitled to remain on the rolls.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Now, take the -- take the exhibit if the Government (Inaudible) his brief on page -- open at page 42. Mr. Cousins and if does A do not computed correctly on application, age do not considered correctly on application, well that's a -- that's an arithmetic calculation, isn't it?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Now, and so, no affidavit as Justice Whittaker suggested could -- could change that correct challenge if that would be a correct challenge, could it?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: I would say that it is conceivable that it could, if it please Justice Frankfurter for the --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But just as you give (Inaudible) would you?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: I would say that this man who has been challenged could file three counter affidavits of persons who would calculate the age the way this Registrar has calculated.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: You mean, calculate erroneously and say that the division as to arithmetic was carried.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Your Honor, Mr. Justice Frankfurter if it please the Court, this is an allegation by a challenger. This is not the decision of the Registrar.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Oh, but he --

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: This is not the decision of the Registrar. This is the an -- this is the opinion of the challenger.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But wouldn't the correctness or error of his claim be something to be verified or discredited by just looking at the dates he gives.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Well, that's correct. That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And if that charges is sustained age not computed correctly, all sorts people make mistakes, I believe half of the human race if half the human race (Inaudible) make mistakes in calculating their ages.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: That is correct if it pleases the Court, but that would not be a question for this Registrar to decide. He would merely try to ascertain the correctness of the traversing affidavits which were presented by the present challenge. This is --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: This (Inaudible) on it's face it's wrong does it, mathematically speaking. If --if --

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: If Your Honor Please.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: If it does, how could he get three people to come up in (Inaudible)

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Did you have Registrars in Louisiana who would take that direction and say, “Well, we were wrong and collected.” Stating it corrected first, we have three witnesses who said that's right we accept it.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: May it please the Court at -- at the state -- the state court have held and I think we have some decisions to bear that out that the question of computing age is a fact that must be met by some six states in literacy test and if the courts, the states require that, then of course they will have to comply with it.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Does the -- (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Did I -- I'm sorry. I would try to just to come back to the question put by Justice Black. You start here with the findings of the District Court that these people were excluded solely on the ground of color. That's what the District Court found.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: You say the evidence doesn't support it. Therefore, we should overturn the finding. Supposing we disagree with you on that and to say that we cannot overturn that finding because there is sufficient evidence that it is supported. Where do you go from there in your position?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Your Honor, if it please the Court, I would certainly -- first -- that even though you might --

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: That's your case, isn't it? I mean that's -- that's the essence of this case leaving out of all this discussion about what the Registrar can do and can't do, you start with findings of the District Court, these people were discriminated against on account of color and only color and you either have to persuade us to overturn those findings or you'll have to show us why if we have accept the findings notwithstanding that the District Court ought to be reversed. That's the whole case, isn't it?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Well, if it please the Court, of course we questioned the jurisdiction of the matter principally, because we think there is no sufficient case here initially -- this -- remember this action was brought because these challengers had made t -- had made this challenge and the Registrar had to accept the challenge. Of course, that would – we cannot deny that the Registrar is state action, but as to the other individuals who unfortunately are not before this Court today, we doubt the state action on their part. Of course, we put the same reasons as this Court has under the decision now in the Raines case attack the constitutionality of the Civil Rights Act itself, because of the broadness of particularly of section -- Subsection A, thereof --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Assume -- assume that as to everything else from the case, you start from their names that the finding is not overturned that they were stricken off the road because of color and the only thing that they would then you confront in opinion (Inaudible) Attorney General namely, that the -- only the -- only the Registrar before the Court and not these private individuals. Do you find any embarrassment, any difficulty in sustaining paragraphs 3 and 4 of the decree? Could that --

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: I would --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- would commerce not be subjected to what Judge Wright their decree?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: I would think that to find the decree is an error principally --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Yes. I don't know, but I'm addressing myself to the question whether the absence of the council people makes the difference.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: I -- I would say that it does make a difference to as to the jurisdictional standpoint --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: -- if it pleases the Court, because there was the action on a part of those individual was individual action and not state action.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Yes, but assume you're right about that. Assume couldn't the proceeding have been began merely against Thomas?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Possibly, it could have actually -- It could have been drawn against Thomas.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap)

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: He had been the man who had done -- completed the act --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, he asked on that. I mean, he is the only follow who really controls whether the name should stay on or be stricken from the roll, isn't it?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And he's decreed in three and four to disregard these challenges by other people to be sure but disregard them and restore their names to the roll. Now, is there any impediment to this caring out that direction or decree of the Court?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: It would be an impediment. It would be a violation of the laws of the State of Louisiana. These people having been taken off of the rolls immediately, if it please the Court, I'd like to mention that if this did not happen in one day, this is over a period about one year that these various challenges were made. And incidentally, I think it's significant that the Court take into consideration the fact that the Government in this matter while they claim 1377 persons, there was actually evidence as to only 155 presented down in the court below.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, I'll take your word for that, a hundred and whatever it is, but 3 (a) says, he -- he's enjoined from giving any legal effect to the approximately fake challenges while in the office. Now, he could carry that out without any unimpeded by the absence of the -- of the -- what do you call consider these council, couldn't he?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Oh, yes. If -- if not --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Excuse me.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I beg your pardon.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: If it please the Court, Mr. Justice Frankfurter, he could not under Louisiana law you say.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, I'm --I'm -- no. I'm now speaking merely to the point of the absence of the citizen's counsel. Forget about Louisiana law --

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- and forget everything else. The fact that the citizen's counsel is not before this Court, is unrelated to the --

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- power of the Court to arrest Thomas to carry out 3 (a), is that right?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Entirely so. Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Equally it's on 3 (b).

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Alright.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: May it please the Court, but we are prepared to contend that this decree goes much further than it possibly ever should have.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, it can't go any further from the specific term of Judge Wright's language, can it?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Well, we contend that the -- the language is in error on -- on the findings of fact and conclusions of law therein --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But, for reasons other than the absence of the city counsil, of the --

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Yes. That is correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: If it please the Court, the reasons other than absence of the citizen's council --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Cousins, that little -- little troubled by this affidavit of Affoway Smiths and that it was -- she was taken out the road because she made a mistake to say in her age. What was the mistake? How much of the mistake did she made?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Your Honor, if it please the Court, I haven't examined each one of these.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: But it's on --

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: (Voice Overlap)

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: -- it's on page 42(a). It's one of this -- it's one of these in the Government's brief, the first one.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Would you mind figuring it out for us and let's us know how much you saw?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: You Honor if you're placing yourself in the position of the Registrar --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I mean, I'm talking about the date there.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: The (Inaudible) registry--

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I want to see. I -- I have definition in the Government, I might figure -- I might figure it out when (Inaudible)

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Do you know that under the Louisiana law, the Registrar may not help a registrant so if you're approaching me under a position of a registrant, I would not be able to give you the help you're asking. Now, I thought that was your point in asking the question.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Oh, no, no. I -- I just don't quite understand.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: If Your Honor please, I find one year.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: One year?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: One day in error. That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: One day?

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: One day error out of 70 year there's 70 years and that -- that you take her off rolls court.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: I beg pardon?

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: And that you take her out for the rolls court.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: If it please the Court, I did not take her off the rolls. She was taken off the rolls --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) -- your state statute.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: No. The state -- the state didn't cause it that she be taken off, but she caused himself to be taken off the rolls by not responding to the challenge.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: I could see --

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: That's our contention.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- she responded if she made a day's mistake, I think she denied.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Your Honor, if it please, but I'd like to mention something else to this Court, if she responded to the challenge she would be told what was wrong with her -- with the calculation.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Would that cause have to be forgiven from the -- the one day (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: She -- the very next day, she could go in and then register correctly. There is no deprivation of a voting right here, it was the whole crops of this thing. There is no deprivation of a voting right.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: In other words, you're telling us that this woman would have no trouble if she could go back there right now and tell him she's wrong and get registered tomorrow.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: That's exactly right. Everyday since these matters came up, she could go -- any -- any day she could had gone back.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Alright.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: That's correct. And as a matter of fact it --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: By the way Mr. Cousins, was she one day older and one day younger than --

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: I had one day older.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: She should had been one day older?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: That's correct. Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) the error isn't it? Then she come in and said she calculated her age on accounting June the 4th the date that she filed the application.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Your Honor if it please, I think that would be up to the each Registrar and if you used the standard system for everyone, whether he use -- included the day of registration or whether he excluded the date of registration would have to be a standard thing that he would apply to all registrants.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: What I meant did she say that?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Well.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: She'll say well I am here (Inaudible) June 4th, I knew it's going to be one more day, but I thought, you didn't count June 4.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: I think your --

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: (Inaudible) is right.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: If the standard work to count the -- the day as worked by the office and as set out by the office. Now, I think she'd be obliged to comply with the standard set by the office for registration, it's part of the literacy test.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Would you mind referring me to the precise language of your Louisiana statute which imposes an absolute and (Inaudible) registrar as to abate under the law to remove a person from the roll, who makes a one day mistake in calculating the number of days he has lived.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Mr. Justice Black. Nothing, I am personally trying to evade the question.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap)

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Mr. Robert has -- has an opportunity, he has the all of the Louisiana law to cover with you, and if it please the Court as much as my time expired.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) -- part of the statute on which you rely about saying that he has no discretion in the case of that kind.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: That he has no discretion in the case, I think that this statute does not set out a specific standard to the day.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) -- you referred to and that's the (Inaudible)

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: Sections 244 and 245. 245 is the one you most nearer to what you're referring to, isn't it?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Well, you're speaking about the challenges of affidavit, now as to the literacy test there are many statutes, the constitution also as to the literacy test.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: What about the discretion of the Registrar?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: The discretion of the Registrar actually comes out of the language of the case which she filed against the -- these very persons who were the cause of this action. Now, the citizen's council had began to issue many challenges against him and he in attempt to avoid either to work or to keep these people on rolls filed a suit in a 23rd Judicial District Court asking of declaratory judgment and injunction against these persons from issuing these citations and affidavits against him. And therein, the Court told him that the only time he had no discretion in the matter, he must necessarily accept the challenge that only time, any discretion on his part might come into play is when he considered the affidavits to traverse the original -- original affidavit of challenge.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Am I right in thinking that the District Court Judge Wright did not concern himself as to whether the particular challenges were good or bad or whether the District -- whether the registrar did or didn't have jurisdiction that all he found was that whenever the state law, it's application had not been applied equally to Negroes and whites.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: That's he whole thing he found.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: He found (Inaudible) discrimination.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: So, we're not really concern with anything except the sufficiency of discriminatory application.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Well, if it please the Court, now, I would say discriminatory application -- I'm reluctant to use the word application. I would prefer using discriminatory use of the law by certain individual citizens if it pleases the Court. But the application of the law stated by the Solicitor General on the part of the Registrar of Voters was not misapplied. There were 1377 colored persons challenged. There were 10 white persons challenged. In each instance, the Registrar did his mandatory duty and issued the citations against all point issues. So as to him, there was no misapplication of the law.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: But he also -- but he also testified did he not, that even though there were only 10 challenges to white persons, he, the Registrar knew that 50% of the registrations of white persons had the same character of infirmity under state law as the 10 challenges exhibited.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Your Honor if it please the Court, the testimony does not bear out that statement as -- as it was quoted to the Court here. The -- the Registrar of Voters was asked that question at the time of deposition of judge was made for the question for the reason that this man had not had a chance to go over the Courts. And frankly, we -- it's not in a testimony. We feel the only time in knew about -- this was after this arose and there, he was asked about how many of the white people and he said “About 50%.” He did not say there was this. He didn't know specifically of the white discrepancies.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Could you -- could readily turn to the actual statement of the Registrar, the question and answer?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: It is on page 50 and 51 of the transcript please.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But you said (Inaudible) about that.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: About, that's correct. I would not say that he was in positive knowledge of that.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Inaudible) relevance you have in concern to our problem, would make 40% of 47% or 55%.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Cousins, may I ask you this question, laying the challenges aside for the moment, laying the challenges completely aside, does the Registrar have either the right or the duty to purge the rolls voters who have made mistakes for which they might be challenged in the manner they were challenged here?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: He has a duty to do it every four years, if it please the court.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: And, well --

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: He should do it (Inaudible) up to date all the time.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: He's supposed to keep them up to date all the time? And -- and to remover any that he -- that he knows or almost arose illegally. Is that right?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: And as to the judgment of the District Court and so forth. Many of these application is obvious from the reading from these applications which were photocopied by the Government that they -- that Registrars evidently helped many of these colored people to get on the rolls. If there was someone in error, he was in error but not to a discriminatory purpose.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: But the thing was going through my mind was this. If having the -- the duty to remove from the rolls, people who were not legally registered, he sat by and did nothing at that kind knowing as he said here that 50% of the white people were subject to the same infirmities that these colored people were subject to and then he left the white councils come in and make affidavit to challenging 98% or whatever it was, or 85% of all the -- all the colored voters and he did nothing about the 50% of the 11,000 white voters who were in exactly the same position. Isn't that in itself a state discrimination?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: If it pleases the Court, answering Mr. Chief Justice, I would like to bring to attention to the Court that before these challenges were issued, there was 90% colored people illegally on the rolls, now, there's only 50% white people illegally on the rolls. But before these challenges were issued, they too did not qualify and they were 9 -- they represent 90% of the colored vote on the roll.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: So, there certainly cannot be said they're been discriminate against by having been left on the rolls in a -- ineligible position and probably would still have been there if the citizen's council hadn't initiated the challenge.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: But if he took off for any reason whether through his -- through his own responsibility, his own duty, or whether through challenges that he took off them off rolls 85% of all the Negroes were registered and left on -- on the rolls, 50% of the -- of the white voters knowing that they have the same infirmities where it was his duty to take them off, wouldn't that in itself be discrimination by the state?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Your Honor if it please, we would like at this moment, if it please the Court to get rid of this Registrar of Voters but we could mot because this matter pending. He unquestionably has been relax and not competent in his duty. These people should never have gotten on the rolls, the whites or the colored -- colored who were unqualified. However, nothing was done, nothing will be done until this matter is determined. He unquestionably only eligible qualified voters in the state rebelled against this thing. Well here, we have so many unqualified people on the roll. Each unqualified vote nullifies both of the qualified persons. So, certain the white -- the qualified persons do not whether they're white or colored to be on the rose anymore. This is just a practical political thing that has been done somewhere along the line. These people got on the roll when it rolls, when they weren't entitled to and the machinery of the state allowed just such a purge as was the challenging system is put in not for purge but to protect the rolls from this sort of practical politics being executed in the state.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, have there any -- is there any parish in the state where they have taken off vast numbers of white voters?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: There it might sound please --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Where?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: If it please, the parish of New Orleans is practically, many of the parishes have white -- white purges Washington Parish, Orleans Parish.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: For what purpose?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Political purposes.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) -- and I mean --

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Various reason, any reason, the same reasons that there on this Court.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, it is one thing if a man moves out of the district, it's a one -- one thing if he's dead. It's another thing -- another thing if he -- if he does something else, if he's convicted of crime perhaps but for things of this kind.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Those are identical reasons if it may please the Court that they use in every instance to get white people off the rolls as well colored people of the rolls. It's a practical political maneuver. It is not discrimination when it's against to white people. He had to tell he has a discrimination once against the colored people, but the same system is used all over the state as against whites by one political fraction as against the other political fraction.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: May I ask you – I beg your pardon?

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Do I understand you to tell us that this is merely political pride to take the voters off against the (Inaudible)

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: I would say it very definitely could be Your Honor. I am not to pass on --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap)

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: -- a discrimination. I beg pardon?

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I understood you to say it was. Am I wrong about that?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: I say it is done as political matter all over the state. It's a practical political matter. We have the CCDA. We'll get against the RDO voters when they think they have people who are not registered properly on the rolls and the various fractions throughout the state will use this very law to purge the rolls of voters who -- when they -- when political passion might get each Registrar of Voters in there who will be over solicitors and over generous towards a Registrar and get the roll chuck full of people who are not -- normally not be qualified because there is a vote block to be had there.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Since this case was tried, the only -- the only one other question, since this case was tried, it is developed the 50% of the white voters are wrongfully on the ballot, has your registrar removed any those 50 percent?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: We would not let him do anything until we get this matter to settle.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: You're not going to do it.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: I beg pardon? I say that we would not let him do something. Frankly, I do not know at this moment, if it please the Court. Personally I -- I have no knowledge with that.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: May I ask you apart from this litigation, is there any authority -- who has authority if anybody has, the Attorney General to initiate purging the rolls of improper registrants?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: The Attorney General does not have that authority.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Anybody?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: The registrar --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- that involve, that-- all have to be.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: The Registrar of Voters himself and other qualified registered voters.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: It merely says that the motion must derived from a citizen --

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: Or the registrar himself.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: The registrar himself. So, that -- so that if there are -- if these improper -- if these 1300 -- 1300 colored people who were improperly who were stricken but improperly stricken because the same impediment, the same disqualification related to white voters who remained on the roll if they were restored, the registrar could take both colored and white off, couldn't he?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Provided he operated with (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: So that restoring this -- the illegal as you claim, there would be illegal voter --

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- would merely mean, that he would have to protect these off but also take the whites off in equal measure, is that right?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That could be done.

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: So that the state isn't impudent, it doesn't have to wait if some private citizen begins to move against particular people, is that's right?

turns.name: WELDON A. COUSINS
turns.text: That is right, thank you.

turns.name: HENRY J. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Roberts.

turns.name: HENRY J. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: -- and the Associate Justice if could take just a little bit of a time to see if I could explain exactly the situation we have here to see if I can bring this Article 245 into focus as to exactly how did they use in this instance. For instance, under the Louisiana registration system, the talk today on 245 is a challenging system opposed to the fact that these registered voters at one time had been a member of voting society because they were illegally placed upon the rolls. Now reason why we say that is that under the constitution of Louisiana, we have a specific requirement as to be a registered voter. The constitution which is set out in our brief says, that you must be at least 21, you must live in the State of Louisiana or locale for certain amount of time and more importantly, you must be a registered voter. Now, by being a registered voter, you must obey the requirements set out is both the constitution and in the legislative acts. Now, as this situation is presently developed, what has happen here in Washington Parish is that these people originally, whenever qualified, they should never have been placed upon the rolls, whether they be white or whether they be black because under Louisiana law, under the constitution, the filling of the registration blank itself is a literacy test. Now, if you fail to pass the literacy test, that is the only time, the only time that the Registrar has any discretion. He has no discretion whatsoever under 245. Under 245 two registered voters come in and they say that they want to challenge me because I have done something wrong, I have misspelled a word, the registrar must -- under the Louisiana law, issue that challenge. He sends that challenge to me and it's up to me to get three registered voters to come in and attempt to traverse that affidavit. You ask me how I will traverse it, supposing I spelled the name of Louisiana wrong, suppose and I spell it L-U-Z-I-A-N-A and suppose I bring three registered voters with me who think that I have spelled Louisiana right who tell me, “I agree, that's the way you spell Louisiana, L-U-Z-I-A-N-A” and my Registrar at that time has no discretion. He has to accept the fact that you spell Louisiana, L-U-Z-I-A-N-A.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Even though you do not?

turns.name: HENRY J. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Even though you do not.[Laughter] Now, wait let me -- let me bring my point. The point B that the Registrar does not have the discretion to determine how to spell Louisiana, the only time he has that discretion is when he is registering an individual for the first time.

turns.name: JUSTICE DOUGLAS
turns.text: But at that kind of a case came up at Louisiana or any other state try to be (Inaudible) literacy test, we certainly would never uphold that claim, would we?

turns.name: HENRY J. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: I think in --

turns.name: JUSTICE DOUGLAS
turns.text: Everybody makes mistakes in writing and spelling.

turns.name: HENRY J. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: That -- that's right, but I think it is up for the State of Louisiana Court, the Court in Louisiana to tell you how to spell Louisiana.

turns.name: JUSTICE DOUGLAS
turns.text: I know but I mean, assuming the first who makes mistakes, we would never sustain that as a literacy test, could we?

turns.name: HENRY J. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: I don't -- I don't think you would sustain it as a literacy test because it's unreasonable.

turns.name: JUSTICE DOUGLAS
turns.text: We never held that the -- it was never held the state have a spelling?

turns.name: HENRY J. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: No. The Supreme Court of Louisiana has held this in a case cited by us, Bishop versus Shipburn, the very fact in miscalculation of age, that very fact has been held to disqualify a person from registering.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: May I ask you on -- on that? Suppose a man is born in 1910 and he registered in 1958 and he puts on his registration that he is 65-year-old.

turns.name: HENRY J. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: He made a mistake. Can he then excuse that by getting three people to swear that he was 65 even though he is really 58?

turns.name: HENRY J. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Your Honor, let me say this. I think that man can.(Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: He can do that. In other words, the Registrar has to take the word of these three witnesses?

turns.name: HENRY J. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: That's right sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: That from 1910 to 1958 is 69 years.

turns.name: HENRY J. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Well, of course Your Honor, that -- that example there is -- is as ridiculous as some of the things that may occur in a registration. For instance --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, I'm -- I'm just trying to find out. I thought you were saying that, that if they made a mistake in calculation, they can get somebody to come and swear that it was a mistake --

turns.name: HENRY J. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: HENRY J. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Your Honor I say this, that if the two people who come in with that -- with three people coming in with that affidavit to indicate that they agree that the man is -- they can't subtract 58 from a certain age and get an idea, how old the man is they won't know either. The point is that these applications are on the rolls right now. We do know that our Registrar didn't know how to calculate.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: You mean your Registrar?

turns.name: HENRY J. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: That's right sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Can he vote?[Laughter]

turns.name: HENRY J. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Disqualification that it is, that's where the 222.[Laughter]

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: We'll recess now.
